core_meaning: Choosing between 'tu' (informal) and 'vous' (formal) based on relationship and context
primary_communicative_fn: Establishing appropriate social distance and respect
when_to_use: When addressing someone in conversation; tu with friends/family, vous with strangers/authority/plural
pattern: tu + verbe conjugué (familier) / vous + verbe conjugué (formel/courtois/pluriel)
essential_vocabulary_slots: tu, vous, veux, voulez, ami, cliente, étudiant, professeur
common_learner_mistakes: Using tu with strangers or authority figures
Using vous with close friends causing distance
Not adjusting register appropriately
chunk_compatibility: Compatible with greetings (*Bonjour, tu vas bien?*), offers (*Tu prends un café?*)
primary_function: Managing social relationships through appropriate pronoun choice
key_forms: Tu veux? / Vous voulez? / On y va? (informal nous)
essential_vocabulary: tu, vous, ami, famille, client, professeur, étranger, respecter
why_it_matters: Register choice is foundational to French politeness and cultural appropriateness; wrong register damages relationships
common_mistakes: Defaulting to tu everywhere (→ perceived rudeness); never switching to vous even when required

structure_label: Intonation vs est-ce que for questions
core_meaning: Using rising intonation instead of 'est-ce que' or inversion in casual speech
primary_communicative_fn: Asking questions informally without formal structure
when_to_use: In casual conversations, spoken French among friends; avoid in writing or formal settings
pattern: intonation montante (rising tone) / n'est-ce pas? (confirmation tag)
key_variations: Tu viens? (instead of Est-ce que tu viens?)
Il est là? (instead of Est-il là?)
essential_vocabulary_slots: viens, est, arrive, sais, comprends, veux
common_learner_mistakes: Using in formal writing
Not recognizing this as valid colloquial French
Applying intonation strategy to formal contexts
chunk_compatibility: Works with social questions (*Tu viens demain?*), confirmations (*Il est là, non?*)
primary_function: Rapid, informal questioning in spoken discourse
key_forms: Tu viens? / Il arrive? / Tu sais? / Ça va?
essential_vocabulary: venir, arriver, savoir, aller, comprendre, vouloir
why_it_matters: Distinguishes formal/written French from natural spoken French; essential for listening comprehension
common_mistakes: Writing intonation-based questions in formal essays; over-formalizing casual speech

core_meaning: Using 'on' instead of 'nous' in informal French; always verb third-person singular
primary_communicative_fn: Including the speaker in a group action while maintaining informal tone
when_to_use: In casual conversation; on is nearly universal in spoken French, nous more in writing/formal
pattern: on + verbe (3e pers. sing.) = nous + verbe conjugué
key_variations: On y va (instead of Nous y allons)
On a dit que (instead of Nous avons dit que)
On peut faire ça (instead of Nous pouvons faire ça)
essential_vocabulary_slots: on, aller, avoir, pouvoir, faire, dire, venir, vivre
common_learner_mistakes: Using 'on' in formal writing
Conjugating verb in plural after 'on'
Forgetting 'on' can mean 'people/one' broadly, not just 'we'
chunk_compatibility: Core to casual activity chunks (*On y va*, *On peut*, *On a*)
primary_function: Casual self-inclusion in group actions; universal in modern French speech
key_forms: On y va / On a dit / On peut faire / On va manger
essential_vocabulary: on, aller, faire, avoir, pouvoir, venir, dire, manger
why_it_matters: On is MANDATORY for natural French speech; learners not exposed sound artificial
common_mistakes: Insisting on 'nous' in casual contexts (→ overly formal); conjugating plural after 'on'

core_meaning: Using 'en' to replace de + noun in formal/academic writing; pronoun replacing quantity or possession
primary_communicative_fn: Avoiding repetition of nouns in formal discourse; maintaining conciseness
when_to_use: In formal writing, academic texts, literature; rare in casual speech
key_variations: J'ai appris la nouvelle → J'en ai appris
Des fleurs? Il y en a beaucoup
Vous avez du pain? J'en veux deux
essential_vocabulary_slots: en, avoir, vouloir, avoir besoin, parler, apprendre, connaître
common_learner_mistakes: Overusing in speech where prepositional phrase is clearer
Using when clarity requires full noun
Misplacing 'en' in compound tenses
chunk_compatibility: Formal requests (*J'en veux trois*), necessity expressions (*On en a besoin*)
primary_function: Formal economy of language; pronoun substitution to avoid noun repetition
key_forms: J'en ai / J'en veux / J'en parle / Je n'en sais rien
essential_vocabulary: en, avoir, vouloir, parler, besoin, fleur, pain, café, temps
why_it_matters: Essential for formal French reading/writing; distinguishes formal register from casual
common_mistakes: Using 'en' in casual speech (→ sounds stilted); forgetting position in compound tenses

core_meaning: Using 'veuille(z)' or passive forms for polite formal commands instead of direct imperative
primary_communicative_fn: Making requests sound extremely polite and indirect in formal contexts
when_to_use: Formal letters, professional requests, diplomatic communication
pattern: Veuillez + infinitif / Que cela soit fait!
key_variations: Veuillez agréer mes salutations distinguées (formal letter)
Que cela soit fait immédiatement! (passive imperative)
essential_vocabulary_slots: veuillez, agréer, faire, être, salutations, contacter, trouver
common_learner_mistakes: Using imperative 'fais' in formal letters
Over-formal tone in casual contexts
Incorrect conjugation of 'vouloir' as 'veuille'
chunk_compatibility: Professional email/letter closings; formal written requests
primary_function: Extremely polite formal imperative; indispensable for professional French
key_forms: Veuillez agréer / Veuillez trouver / Que cela soit fait
essential_vocabulary: veuillez, agréer, salutations, distinguées, contacter, joindre, trouver
why_it_matters: Mandatory in French formal correspondence; omission perceived as rude or casual
common_mistakes: Using direct imperative in formal letters; incorrect 'veille' conjugation

structure_label: Passive voice register: formal vs active
core_meaning: Passive voice (être + participe passé) is more frequent in formal/written French than active
primary_communicative_fn: Shifting focus from agent to action; creating formality or impersonality
when_to_use: Academic texts, formal reports, official documents; rare in spoken French
pattern: être + participe passé (+ par + agent optional)
key_variations: La lettre a été écrite (passive, formal)
On a écrit la lettre (active, neutral)
essential_vocabulary_slots: être, participe passé, par, rapport, lettre, soumettre, écrire, réaliser
common_learner_mistakes: Overusing passive in speech (sounds unnatural)
Avoiding passive in formal writing when appropriate
Confusion about past participle agreement in passive
chunk_compatibility: Technical/academic reports (*Le projet a été complété*), formal announcements
primary_function: Formal/academic tone; focusing on action rather than agent
key_forms: Sera écrit / A été dit / Est considéré / Peuvent être trouvés
essential_vocabulary: être, participe passé, par, rapport, lettre, projet, système, résultat
why_it_matters: Passive voice is obligatory register marker for formal French; overuse in speech sounds artificial
common_mistakes: Using passive excessively in conversation; incorrect past participle agreement

core_meaning: Common informal contractions in spoken French; dropping syllables or eliding words
primary_communicative_fn: Rapid, authentic speech patterns in casual conversation
when_to_use: Casual conversation, listening comprehension; NEVER in writing
pattern: je → j' / ne → ∅ / il → y / lui → 'lui / pas → pa
key_variations: je lui ai dit → j'lui ai dit
essential_vocabulary_slots: sais, avoir, lui, dire, vouloir, pouvoir, aller, besoin
common_learner_mistakes: Using in writing (unacceptable in formal/standard contexts)
Not recognizing in listening comprehension
Overusing to sound 'authentic' (→ sounds forced)
chunk_compatibility: Casual acknowledgment chunks (*Y'a pas de souci*), informal speech (*Chais pas*)
primary_function: Natural rapid speech; listening comprehension of native speakers
key_forms: Chais pas / Y'a pas / J'lui ai dit / Faut / Veux
essential_vocabulary: sais, avoir, lui, vouloir, pouvoir, faire, aller, jour
why_it_matters: Critical for understanding authentic French speech; distinguishes formal writing from natural conversation
common_mistakes: Writing contractions in essays; not recognizing them in listening

core_meaning: Using appropriate formulaic expressions in formal correspondence (opening, body, closing)
primary_communicative_fn: Establishing professional tone and following French epistolary norms
pattern: Monsieur/Madame + objet + corps + formules de politesse standardisées
key_variations: Je vous prie d'agréer, Madame, l'expression de mes salutations distinguées
essential_vocabulary_slots: Monsieur, Madame, Directeur, agréer, salutations, distinguées, attente, cordialement
common_learner_mistakes: Using informal closings ('bisous') in professional letters
Mixing registers mid-letter
Omitting required formulas (perceived as rude)
chunk_compatibility: Professional opening/closing chunks; formal written requests
primary_function: Establishing professional credibility through conventional epistolary form
key_forms: Monsieur, / Je vous prie d'agréer / Mes salutations / Cordialement
essential_vocabulary: Monsieur, Madame, agréer, salutations, distinguées, prier, cordialement, attendre
why_it_matters: French letter writing is highly conventional; deviation is perceived as incompetent or disrespectful
common_mistakes: Informal tone in professional letters; missing or incorrect closing formula

core_meaning: Using field-specific terminology appropriately in professional contexts
primary_communicative_fn: Demonstrating expertise and communicating precisely within professional domains
when_to_use: Professional meetings, reports, specialized discussions; avoid with non-experts
pattern: terme technique + contexte professionnel / facture vs note
essential_vocabulary_slots: facture, réunion, collègue, rapport, projet, délai, budget, contrat
common_learner_mistakes: Using technical jargon with non-experts (mismatched audience)
Using overly simple terms in professional settings
Mixing English business terms inappropriately
chunk_compatibility: Professional communication (*Voici la facture*, *Réunion prévue demain*)
primary_function: Precise professional communication; demonstrating field competence
key_forms: La facture / rapport mensuel / délai imparti / contrat signé
essential_vocabulary: facture, réunion, rapport, projet, délai, budget, contrat, signature
why_it_matters: Professional register credibility depends on precise terminology; wrong level damages professional ethos
common_mistakes: Translating English jargon literally; using casual terms in formal reports

core_meaning: Using impersonal verbs and hedging language in academic French writing
primary_communicative_fn: Creating objectivity, distance, and scholarly authority; avoiding direct assertion
when_to_use: Academic papers, research writing, scholarly discourse
pattern: il semble que / il paraît que / il convient de / il importe de + clause
key_variations: On peut affirmer que... (slightly less formal than il importe)
essential_vocabulary_slots: semble, paraît, convient, importe, noter, affirmer, souligner, phénomène
common_learner_mistakes: Being too direct/assertive in academic writing
Using colloquial expressions in papers
Overusing personal pronouns (je/nous) in formal academic texts
chunk_compatibility: Academic introductions (*Il convient de noter*), literature review (*Il semble que*)
primary_function: Creating scholarly register; asserting ideas with appropriate academic hedging
key_forms: Il semble / Il convient / Il importe / On peut affirmer
essential_vocabulary: semble, convient, importe, noter, affirmer, souligner, phénomène, argument
why_it_matters: Academic register is essential for credibility in scholarly French; directness damages ethos
common_mistakes: Using personal voice ('Je crois'); omitting hedging ('C'est vrai que')

structure_label: Tu/vous across generations and social evolution
core_meaning: Evolution of tu/vous usage among different age groups and modern social contexts
primary_communicative_fn: Navigating generational norms in French address; understanding social change
when_to_use: Assessing appropriate register in diverse social situations; cross-generational communication
pattern: tu (jeunes/entre-famille) / vous (aînés/professionnel) / shifting boundaries by generation
key_variations: Les jeunes se tutoient entre eux (even strangers may tu)
Dans cette famille, on se vouvoie encore (older families retain vous)
En entreprise moderne: tu entre collègues; vous avec clients
essential_vocabulary_slots: jeunes, âinés, famille, entreprise, collègue, client, génération, usage
common_learner_mistakes: Assuming all French people your age use tu everywhere
Not adjusting to generational norms
Overgeneralizing Parisian youth norms to all groups
chunk_compatibility: Contextual greeting adjustments (*Tu vas bien?* vs *Vous allez bien?*)
primary_function: Sociolinguistic competence; reading generational/regional context appropriately
key_forms: Variable by group; no fixed pattern; context-dependent
essential_vocabulary: tu, vous, génération, jeune, âiné, usage, social, moderne
why_it_matters: Tu/vous boundaries are shifting in modern French; rigid adherence may seem outdated
common_mistakes: Refusing to tu younger people even when invited; excessive vous with peers

core_meaning: Recognizing that register norms and vocabulary vary by French-speaking region
primary_communicative_fn: Understanding regional identity markers; avoiding over-normalization
when_to_use: When traveling or reading literature from other Francophone regions
pattern: regional forms ≠ standard Parisian French; valid alternatives
key_variations: Canadian: different tu/vous boundaries; 'là' as discourse marker
African French: distinct vocabulary and pronunciation
essential_vocabulary_slots: septante, huitante, soixante-dix, quatre-vingts, là, Belgique, Suisse, Canada
common_learner_mistakes: Thinking Parisian French is the only standard
Using regional markers in inappropriate formal contexts
Judging non-Parisian variants as 'wrong'
chunk_compatibility: Situational adaptability; understanding regional media and literature
primary_function: Sociolinguistic awareness; respecting regional Francophone identity
essential_vocabulary: septante, huitante, soixante-dix, quatre-vingts, Belgique, Suisse, Canada, Afrique
why_it_matters: French is pluricentric language; learners must recognize valid regional variation
common_mistakes: Insisting on Parisian norms; dismissing regional variants; rigid standardization

core_meaning: Using negative/subjunctive structures to soften requests or refusals politely
primary_communicative_fn: Making requests/refusals more tactful and socially appropriate
pattern: Je ne peux pas... / Je serais désolé de ne pas / Il serait préférable de ne pas
key_variations: Je ne peux pas / Je suis désolé de ne pas pouvoir (instead of 'Non')
Il serait préférable de ne pas... (instead of 'Non, on peut pas')
Je ne pourrais malheureusement pas (hypothetical softening)
essential_vocabulary_slots: pouvoir, désolé, préférable, ne, malheureusement, pouvoir, préférer
common_learner_mistakes: Being too blunt in requests ('Fais-le!'; use subjunctive)
Using imperative when softness is needed
Overusing negativity (→ sounds overly negative)
chunk_compatibility: Polite refusal chunks (*Je ne peux pas*), diplomatic requests
primary_function: Softening refusals/requests through negative polarity and subjunctive mood
key_forms: Je ne peux pas / Il serait préférable / Je serais désolé
essential_vocabulary: pouvoir, désolé, préférable, malheureusement, ne, malheureusement, préférer
why_it_matters: Polite softening is cultural norm in French interaction; directness perceived as rude
common_mistakes: Being too direct ('Non'); using imperative in diplomatic contexts

core_meaning: Using appropriate style for online communication (abbreviations, tone, conventions)
primary_communicative_fn: Communicating efficiently in digital spaces; understanding online identity markers
when_to_use: Social media, text messaging, casual online forums; NEVER in formal contexts
essential_vocabulary_slots: mdr, svp, pk, OMG, lol, chelou, dingue, à bientôt
common_learner_mistakes: Using abbreviations in professional emails
Using formal register in informal online contexts
Over-abbreviating to point of unintelligibility
chunk_compatibility: Casual online exchange chunks (*À plus tard*, *C chelou*)
primary_function: Efficient digital communication; signaling in-group membership online
key_forms: mdr / svp / pk / @+ / OMG / c'est / ya
essential_vocabulary: mdr, svp, pk, OMG, lol, à plus, demain, aujourd'hui, merci
why_it_matters: Digital register is now dominant in many French contexts; understanding conventions is essential
common_mistakes: Using abbreviations in professional context; formal tone in casual digital spaces

core_meaning: Switching registers within a single conversation based on interlocutor/context shift
primary_communicative_fn: Maintaining social fluency across changing contexts; adapting to audience shifts
when_to_use: Any conversation that changes context (formal meeting → casual break; one person leaves/arrives)
key_variations: Au travail avec collègue: vous / Après avec ami: tu
Switching from vous to tu when relationship develops
essential_vocabulary_slots: tu, vous, formel, familier, collègue, ami, réunion, pause
common_learner_mistakes: Staying in one register when context changes
Switching abruptly without transition
Not recognizing when code-switch is appropriate/required
chunk_compatibility: Situational adaptability; multiple register chunks required
key_forms: Variable; depends on context; no fixed forms
essential_vocabulary: tu, vous, formel, familier, context, situation, approprié
why_it_matters: Code-switching skill is mark of fluent French speaker; inability to switch signals non-nativeness
common_mistakes: Failing to recognize register shift opportunities; sudden, jarring transitions
